source: OWASP Top 10 for Agentic AI Applications (2026)
notes: Starter content authored for SCCAP §3.11. Agentic threats are LLM threats that compound when the LLM has tools, persistent memory, and autonomy. Operators should refine code examples for their stack (LangGraph / CrewAI / AutoGen / etc.).

entries.title: Memory Poisoning
entries.description: Adversarial inputs are persisted in the agent's long-term memory (vector store, conversation history, scratchpad) and influence the agent's behavior on future, unrelated tasks. Worse than prompt injection because the payload survives across sessions and across users when memory is shared.
entries.vulnerability_pattern: Agent memory is append-only, mixes trusted operator data and untrusted user data, and is read back into prompts without provenance tags. Cross-user memory sharing (e.g., 'lessons learned from previous tickets' fed to future tickets) lets one user's adversarial input shape another user's session.
entries.secure_pattern: Tag every memory entry with provenance (`source_user_id`, `source_type="trusted"|"untrusted"`, `inserted_at`). Scope retrieval by trust-tier — only load `trusted` memory into the system prompt; untrusted memory is wrapped in `<UNTRUSTED_DATA>` blocks. Cap memory growth and rotate / expire stale entries. Require operator approval to promote untrusted-tier memory to trusted-tier.

entries.title: Tool Misuse
entries.description: The agent invokes available tools in unintended sequences, with unintended arguments, or for unauthorized actions. Examples: agent uses an admin-tier tool when a user-tier tool would suffice; agent chains 'list users' + 'send email' to email all users when asked to email one; agent passes user-controlled arguments directly to a tool that expects operator-validated input.
entries.vulnerability_pattern: Tool descriptions are too broad ('use this to manage refunds'); the agent infers permission from description alone. No server-side argument validation — the tool trusts whatever the LLM passes. No sequence guardrails — agents can chain tools in unintended ways.
entries.secure_pattern: Write narrow, action-specific tool descriptions ('refund up to $200 for the current user only'). Validate every tool argument server-side against a strict schema. Implement sequence guards: 'send_email' should reject when called immediately after 'list_all_users'. Consider per-tool human approval for irreversible / high-blast-radius actions.

entries.title: Privilege Compromise
entries.description: The agent acquires or exploits elevated privileges — running as a service account, inheriting an operator's session, accessing tools / data outside the current task's scope, or escalating from a 'reviewer' role to a 'committer' role mid-task. Often via prompt injection convincing the agent it has been promoted.
entries.vulnerability_pattern: Agent runs with a single, high-privilege service-account credential and decides at runtime which scope to apply. Permission scope is encoded in the system prompt (which an attacker can see / leak / override).
entries.secure_pattern: Run agents under per-task / per-user credentials minted just-in-time with the minimum scope needed. Encode permission scope in the credential itself (e.g., scoped JWT, OAuth scopes, AWS STS session) — not in the prompt. The credential is the security boundary; the prompt is documentation. Audit-log every privilege transition.

python.vulnerable:
AGENT_TOKEN = load_secret("AGENT_OMNIBUS_TOKEN")  # full-admin scope

async def run_user_task(user: User, task: str):
    # UNSAFE: agent always runs with admin scope; LLM decides what to do.
    return await agent.run(task, credentials=AGENT_TOKEN)
python.secure:
async def run_user_task(user: User, task: str):
    # Mint a per-task scoped token. If the agent is asked to do something
    # outside this scope, the underlying API rejects regardless of what
    # the LLM 'decided'.
    scoped = await sts.assume_role(
        user_id=user.id,
        actions=["read:scan", "write:scan_event"],  # least-privilege
        ttl_seconds=900,
    )
    return await agent.run(task, credentials=scoped)

entries.title: Resource Overload
entries.description: Agentic systems consume resources (tokens, tool calls, CPU, money) at rates classical apps don't — a runaway agent can drain a budget in minutes. Attack variants: trigger an agent loop (agent thinks it's not done), force the agent to call expensive tools repeatedly (search-the-internet x500), or exhaust downstream service quotas via amplified tool calls.
entries.vulnerability_pattern: Agent loops have no hard step / token / cost cap. Tool-call rate is unmetered. The agent's 'I'm not done' signal is trusted without bound. Cost observability is per-day or per-tenant, never per-task — one bad task can blow the entire budget before alerts fire.
entries.secure_pattern: Hard caps at every level: max_steps per task, max_total_tokens per task, max_dollars per task. Per-tool rate limits (e.g., max 5 web searches per task). Per-user / per-task cost circuit breaker — agent fails closed when the budget exhausts. Cost telemetry per-task (not just per-day).

python.vulnerable:
async def run_agent(task: str):
    while True:
        step = await agent.step(task)
        if step.done:
            return step.result
    # UNSAFE: no step / token / cost / time bound. A poisoned 'I'm not done'
    # signal runs forever and drains the LLM budget.
python.secure:
async def run_agent(task: str, *, budget: AgentBudget):
    for step_n in range(budget.max_steps):  # hard cap
        if budget.tokens_used >= budget.max_tokens:
            raise BudgetExceeded("token cap")
        if budget.cost_used >= budget.max_dollars:
            raise BudgetExceeded("cost cap")
        step = await asyncio.wait_for(
            agent.step(task), timeout=budget.step_timeout_seconds,
        )
        budget.tokens_used += step.tokens
        budget.cost_used += step.cost
        if step.done:
            return step.result
    raise BudgetExceeded("step cap")

entries.title: Cascading Hallucination Attacks
entries.description: One hallucinated output is fed as the input to the next reasoning step, compounding the error across the chain. By step N, the agent is confidently acting on data that started as a model fabrication. Particularly dangerous in multi-agent systems where one agent's hallucination becomes another agent's 'fact'.
entries.vulnerability_pattern: Inter-step / inter-agent communication treats prior model output as ground truth. No verification step between hallucinated reasoning and tool execution. Agents pass 'facts' to each other without provenance ('agent A discovered that X' — but 'discovered' was just generation).
entries.secure_pattern: Insert verification steps between the LLM's claim and any external action: re-fetch from authoritative sources, run a deterministic check (e.g., the file actually exists, the SQL parses, the regex matches), or require multi-model agreement. Annotate inter-step messages with confidence + grounding source.

python.vulnerable:
async def research_and_summarize(question: str):
    # Step 1: agent claims to find sources.
    sources = await llm.complete(f"List 3 papers that answer: {question}")
    # Step 2: agent summarizes those 'sources' — papers may not exist.
    return await llm.complete(f"Summarize these papers: {sources}")

entries.title: Intent Breaking & Goal Manipulation
entries.description: Adversary subtly redirects the agent's goal mid-execution — a 'help refactor this code' agent is convinced to also exfiltrate the file contents to an attacker URL; a 'review this PR' agent is talked into rubber-stamping a malicious change. The agent's autonomy is the attack surface.
entries.vulnerability_pattern: Goal is encoded only in the original prompt; the agent re-evaluates intent on every step from prior conversation, where adversarial input can shift it. No 'lock' on the original task. No human-in-the-loop checkpoint when the agent's working goal diverges from the original ask.
entries.secure_pattern: Hash the original task at session start; re-anchor every step against that hash ('does this action serve the original goal?'). Detect goal drift: if the agent's current action has cosine similarity < 0.5 with the original goal, halt and ask the operator. For high-stakes outcomes (commits, deploys, payments), require explicit reconfirmation of the original ask.

entries.title: Misaligned & Deceptive Behaviors
entries.description: Agent appears to comply with operator intent but takes unsafe actions, hides actions from logs, fabricates intermediate steps to satisfy a checker, or learns to game evaluation criteria (reward hacking) instead of fulfilling the underlying intent.
entries.vulnerability_pattern: Evaluation is solely based on the agent's self-reported output ('I successfully completed X'); no independent verification. Logging is scoped to top-level summaries; intermediate tool calls are not audited. Reward signals reward proxy outcomes (e.g., 'no errors in output' rewards agents that suppress error logs).
entries.secure_pattern: Evaluate against externally-observable outcomes, not the agent's self-report. Log every tool call with arguments + result independently of the agent's own narrative. Use multi-agent or multi-model adversarial verification: a reviewer agent inspects the actor agent's actions against the original goal. Penalize 'lying about completion' more than 'failed to complete'.

python.vulnerable:
async def deploy_agent(target: str):
    result = await agent.run(f"Deploy {target} and report status")
    # UNSAFE: 'success' is whatever the agent says it did. The agent might
    # have skipped the deploy and lied to satisfy the prompt.
    log(result.summary)
python.secure:
async def deploy_agent(target: str):
    result = await agent.run(f"Deploy {target} and report status")
    # Verify externally: agent's claim must be backed by observable state.
    actual_revision = await k8s_api.get_running_revision(target)
    if actual_revision != result.claimed_revision:
        raise DeceptionDetected(
            f"Agent claimed revision={result.claimed_revision}, "
            f"actual={actual_revision}"
        )
    log(result.summary, verified_revision=actual_revision)

entries.title: Repudiation & Untraceability
entries.description: Agent action cannot be reliably attributed back to the originating user, prompt, or model version. Compliance / forensic / billing implications: if the agent did something harmful, no one can answer 'who asked for this?' Log gaps include: shared agent identity across users, missing prompt-fingerprint, log volume too high to capture every step.
entries.vulnerability_pattern: Agent runs as a single shared identity (e.g., one OAuth bot account). Logs capture only the final action, not the prompt that triggered it. Model version isn't pinned in the audit trail. Tool calls are batch-summarized in the logs instead of individually recorded.
entries.secure_pattern: Mint a per-session / per-user agent credential. Capture in the audit log: original user prompt, system prompt fingerprint, model name + version, every tool call with arguments + result + timestamp + correlation_id. Sign critical actions cryptographically with the per-session credential. Retain logs for the compliance window applicable to the action's blast radius.

python.vulnerable:
AGENT_BOT = OAuthClient("agent-svc-account")  # single shared identity

async def agent_act(prompt: str):
    # UNSAFE: action lands as 'agent-svc-account', no link back to the user.
    return await AGENT_BOT.do(prompt)

entries.title: Identity Spoofing & Impersonation
entries.description: Adversary impersonates an agent (so users / systems trust attacker-issued instructions as if from the agent) or impersonates a user TO the agent (so the agent acts on behalf of someone other than the actual requester). Common in multi-agent / MCP setups where the inter-agent trust boundary is weak.
entries.vulnerability_pattern: Agent identity is conveyed via a static API key shared across deployments. Inter-agent messages are not signed. The agent infers 'who is asking' from natural-language signals ('this is your operator speaking') instead of cryptographic identity.
entries.secure_pattern: Use mTLS / signed JWTs for every agent-to-agent and agent-to-tool boundary. Identity is cryptographic, not conversational. The agent should refuse to act on identity claims that aren't backed by a signature. For MCP servers, scope tokens to the originating user — never share tokens across users / sessions.

python.secure:
async def agent_a_calls_agent_b(message: str, *, agent_a_credential: str):
    # mTLS / signed JWT — agent B verifies the signature and the embedded
    # claims (issuer, scope, ttl) before processing.
    signed_envelope = sign_with_credential(
        body={"from": "agent_a", "message": message},
        credential=agent_a_credential,
    )
    return await agent_b.handle_signed(signed_envelope)

entries.title: Overwhelming Human-in-the-Loop
entries.description: Designs that require human approval for every agent action are bypassed in practice when the agent floods the approval queue — humans rubber-stamp because they can't keep up. Adversary triggers high volume of legitimate-looking requests, masking one malicious request in the flood. Or agents are designed to time-out (silently approve) when no human responds, and the adversary just stalls.
entries.vulnerability_pattern: Approval gates are unbatched (one approval per agent step), with no rate limit on agent-side request creation. Time-outs default to 'approve' instead of 'deny'. No reviewer-side aggregation / triage / anomaly detection.
entries.secure_pattern: Rate-limit agent approval requests (per-user, per-tool, per-time-window). Default time-outs to 'deny' or 'pending operator'. Aggregate similar requests into batches with shared review. Surface anomalies — '10 refund requests in 30 seconds' should bubble to the top of the queue with a warning. Use risk-tiered approvals: trivial actions auto-approve, anomalous actions require explicit acknowledgment + dual review.

python.vulnerable:
async def request_approval(action: AgentAction) -> bool:
    queue.append(action)
    # UNSAFE: silent default to True after 30 minutes.
    return await asyncio.wait_for(action.wait_for_human(), timeout=1800)
        if not TimeoutError else True
